Arthur sat alone on the porch, staring at the heavy rain against the glass. It had been months since he left for the front lines, leaving his wife behind. Their love remained intact despite the distance, sustained by the written word. He reached into his coat pocket and pulled out the folded paper. It was a **letter**, trembling slightly in his fingers, smelling faintly of ink and tobacco.

He traced the familiar handwriting. Clara's script was always neat, a lifeline across the ocean of conflict. He read slowly, memorizing every sentiment she shared. He felt a surge of gratitude that she still cared enough to **letter** home, even amidst the chaos of the world. They were united by the silence of the battlefield and the noise of their correspondence, keeping their connection alive.<|im_end|>

final_answer:
Arthur sat alone on the porch, staring at the heavy rain against the glass. It had been months since he left for the front lines, leaving his wife behind. Their love remained intact despite the distance, sustained by the written word. He reached into his coat pocket and pulled out the folded paper. It was a **letter**, trembling slightly in his fingers, smelling faintly of ink and tobacco.

He traced the familiar handwriting. Clara's script was always neat, a lifeline across the ocean of conflict. He read slowly, memorizing every sentiment she shared. He felt a surge of gratitude that she still cared enough to **letter** home, even amidst the chaos of the world. They were united by the silence of the battlefield and the noise of their correspondence, keeping their connection alive.<|im_end|>
